translation.content: Lore is an underrated quality. Not quite secrecy, more conjuring a sense of mythology that enhances the magnetic pull of the music. Neuroscientists have mapped that sound elicits similar feelings in the brain to when we satiate cravings, but what about the psychological impulse that drives listeners of a certain disposition toward everything they don't know? It's tricky to put your finger on, but artists able to maintain intrigue without overhawking the backstory can cut through—just ask SAULT, [ar:pi:ar] or Gerald Donald. Then there are those who don't try whatsoever. These, you tend to find, are the ones who stay in mind the most. In 2001, a striking 12-inch called Ship-Scope emerged through Chain Reaction, credited to Shinichi Atobe, with scant other info available. OK, mulled fans, this is probably a cat-and-mouse game dreamed up by someone on a label with a fine line in foggy obfuscation. Vainqueur on a wind-up? Another Mark & Moritz alias? But no: Atobe was real, and really had just posted a demo to Hard Wax. It was that simple. Then he returned to his day job—until, after 13 years of silence, an even better follow-up emerged. From 2014's near-perfect Butterfly Effect onwards, Atobe has built up one of the most revered catalogues in underground circles. A steady clip of elegant, transportive dub techno and deep electronics has arrived through Demdike Stare's DDS, complimented by the launch of his own label, plastic & sounds, earlier this summer. The distance was never tactical. When Tone Glow banked his first and, until now, only spot of media last year, it felt like a genuine scoop for an artist thought to be functionally uncontactable. Funnily enough, Atobe had been keen to chat all along, but no one had thought to reach out. (Cue the loud reverberation of palms slapping foreheads.) Atobe has also made strides into the public domain, travelling south from his home of Saitama to grace Tokyo's WWW with his first live performance at the tender age of 52. He'll be back next week to play alongside Loidis and Deadbeat for a killer plastic & sounds party. It's that debut 2023 show you'll hear on RA.1009: a hypnotic, yet comparatively pumping, set full of Atobe material mostly unable to be found anywhere else. In both sound and discretion, Atobe seems to be preserved in amber from some bygone era, which has only spiked the dopamine hit accompanying each release as it lands out the sky. Contact, as you might anticipate, is glacial: since we began exchanging emails, the RA Mix has changed name, look and rolled over into its second millennium. Still, patience pays off. This is a set we're stoked to run. One question lingers: is Atobe aware of his cult status? "I didn't know about this," he told us in typically reduced fashion. "I've just been making the kind of music I like." - Gabriel Szatan How and where was this set recorded? This is an edited recording from my first live performance in the spring of 2023 at the Tokyo venue WWW. Performing your music for a live audience must have been an interesting experience after so long producing privately. Can you tell us more? I was definitely happy about it. I didn't feel nervous at all—didn't even have a beer. If you could convey the mood or tone of each of your albums in a single word or phrase, what would you go for? Butterfly Effect = Winter. World = Ignorance. From The Heart, It's A Start, A Work Of Art = Dawn. Heat = Midsummer. Yes = Early summer. Love of Plastic = Autumn. Discipline = Back to the beginning. How does it make you feel to cultivate a global following, while retaining your privacy? I suppose one thing I can be proud of is that I never tried to turn it into money quickly. Many of your favourite electronic records— from artists such as Round Five, Satoshi Tomiie and Glenn Underground—come from the mid-to-late '90s. What is it about the production choices and songcraft from that period that resonates with you? It's honestly because that was the period when I was buying the most records, and I feel many artists back then had at least some understanding of music theory. Earlier this year, you began plastic & sounds. What made you want to start a label? What are your intentions going forward, and what can we expect from your album in spring 2026? I just felt that maybe it was finally time. From now on, I'd like to take on projects that include vocals as well. The new album will reflect my experiences so far. Some tracks were actually completed two or three years ago, so I think the upcoming works will continue to evolve from here. Finally: What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? They say AI will surpass humans in the next two or three years. Apparently, the kind of human being who will still be respected by AI is one who can act beyond personal gain or loss. We should be sure to remember this.
translation.blurb: The first-ever live recording from an elusive icon of dubby electronic music.
